trajectory_summary: The call moves from neutral compliance to genuine engagement — Carter ends the call asking questions and volunteering an EV, which is a better close than the open. The booking is real, but the trajectory is positive-but-underworked: Varun exited the verify section two anchors short, delivered a thin button-up that confused the customer, and then treated Carter's EV disclosure as a logistics note rather than the bill-pain amplifier it is. The appointment is booked; the closer walks in with less context than the call could have built.
outcome_dispute: The v1 tag is listed as unknown. Based on the transcript, the call ends with a confirmed appointment at 4PM the following day — outcome should be tagged booked. The appointment carries conditional risk (Carter is away from home on weekdays, computer/email access at 4PM is unconfirmed, and Carter's stated preference was weekends), but a time and date were agreed.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Verify completion — interest_in_solar and average_bill anchors
primary_coaching_focus.quote: Cool. Yeah. So that is all the questions that I had.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Varun exits verify without the two items the schema identifies as the emotional core of the pitch. Carter volunteered an EV signal at 03:38 that retroactively confirms bill-pain is real and growing — but Varun never asked what interested Carter in solar or what the bill looks like. The closer inherits a booked appointment with no emotional anchor and no bill baseline. On a new-home lead where bill history is thin, the interest question is even more important, not less — it is the only way to surface motivation before the design call.

key_moments.quote: Hi, Carter. My name is Varun. I actually got a call from me.
key_moments.stewart_read: The intro is garbled — 'I actually got a call from me' is either a transcript artifact or a stumbled line. More importantly, Varun does not establish Ion Solar's name, does not reference the form submission for legitimacy, and does not use the Tesla framing that the schema identifies as the highest-legitimacy opener. The result: Carter has no anchor for who is calling or why this call is legitimate.

key_moments.quote: what's what's the name of the company, sir?
key_moments.stewart_read: Carter asks for the company name four minutes into the call — a direct legitimacy-gap signal. This is not a hostile challenge; it is a customer who genuinely does not know who they are talking to. The intro failed to anchor Ion Solar's identity, and this is the downstream consequence. Varun answers correctly ('Ion Solar') but the damage is already done: the closer inherits a customer who needed to ask.

key_moments.quote: Cool. Yeah. So that is all the questions that I had.
key_moments.stewart_read: Varun exits the verify section without capturing interest_in_solar or average_bill — the two anchors the schema identifies as the emotional core of the pitch. The confirmed misses are these two; prior_designs was addressed at 00:22 and utility company (ComEd) appears at 03:23, suggesting it was known from the lead record. The six-of-eight threshold may still be breached (two confirmed misses out of eight items), but the critical gap is the absence of any emotional anchor. The closer walks in knowing Carter has an EV and a new home but has no bill pain on record and no stated motivation for solar.

Carter's stated preference is weekends. Varun overrides this to book a weekday at 4PM. The override is not unreasonable — Varun correctly clarifies that the appointment is a phone call, not a home visit — but the preference signal is real. A customer booked against their stated preference is a no-show risk, and the handoff brief must flag it.
